statement.markdown: Given a matrix, return True if it can be rearranged to form a square permutation matrix. A permutation matrix is a square matrix obtained by permuting the rows of an identity matrix according to some permutation of integers 1 to n. For example the matrix [[0, 1], [1, 0]] can be obtained from the identity matrix [[1, 0], [0, 1]] by swapping its rows. The given matrix will contain exactly n^2 elements where n is the size of the square, and each element will be between 0 and n^2-1 inclusive, and at most one element will be equal to any integer i in the range [0, n^2-1].
statement.inputFormat:
Input Format:
  The input matrix is given as an array of arrays, where each inner array contains n elements.
  You can assume that the input matrix is already given in a 2D array format.
statement.outputFormat: Your function should return True if the matrix can be rearranged to form a square permutation matrix, False otherwise.
statement.notes: Note that the given matrix can be rearranged into a unique permutation matrix. In other words, there is only one possible permutation matrix that can be obtained from the given matrix.

examples.explanation: The given matrix can be rearranged into the matrix [[1,0],[0,1]]
The given matrix cannot be rearranged into a permutation matrix

hints.text: First hint text: Try to first count the number of occurrences of each element in the matrix.
Second hint text: Once we have the counts, we can try to check if any element has a count greater than 1, or if there are any elements that are missing.

editorial.title: Editorial title: Rearranging a Permutation Matrix
editorial.intuition: Explanation of the correct approach: We can solve this problem by using the concept of permutations. A permutation matrix is a square matrix that has exactly one 1 in each row and column, and 0s elsewhere. To rearrange a permutation matrix, we can simply swap the rows of the matrix to get the desired permutation. In this problem, we need to check if a given matrix can be rearranged into a square permutation matrix. We can do this by counting the number of occurrences of each element in the matrix, and then checking if any element has a count greater than 1, or if there are any elements that are missing. If we find any such element, we can conclude that the matrix cannot be rearranged into a permutation matrix.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach summary: The brute force approach would be to try to rearrange the matrix in all possible ways and check if any of them results in a permutation matrix. However, this approach is highly inefficient and would not be able to solve the problem within the given time limit.
editorial.betterSolution: Better solution details: We can use a two-pointer approach to solve this problem. We can start by counting the number of occurrences of each element in the matrix. Then, we can use two pointers to check if any element has a count greater than 1, or if there are any elements that are missing. If we find any such element, we can conclude that the matrix cannot be rearranged into a permutation matrix.
editorial.optimalSolution: Optimal solution details: We can use a single pass through the matrix to count the number of occurrences of each element and check if any element has a count greater than 1, or if there are any elements that are missing. If we find any such element, we can conclude that the matrix cannot be rearranged into a permutation matrix.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Correctness proof: The correctness of the solution can be proved by showing that the solution is able to correctly identify whether a matrix can be rearranged into a permutation matrix or not.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run: We can use a sample input to run the solution and see the output. For example, if we use the input [[1,0],[0,1]] as the input, the solution should return True.
commonMistakes: Mistake 1: Counting the number of occurrences of each element incorrectly.
Mistake 2: Not checking for elements with count greater than 1, or elements that are missing.
